items.title: Supervised learning
items.description: Regression and classification are the two main supervised problem types — both learn from labeled (x, y) pairs. The supervised paradigm and training loop are defined in the supervised learning topic; this topic splits that paradigm by target type.

items.title: Linear regression
items.description: Linear regression is the foundational regression algorithm: it models ŷ = w·x + b for continuous targets and is trained with squared-error loss. Use when y is numeric and a linear relationship is a reasonable first baseline.

items.title: Logistic regression
items.description: Logistic regression is the foundational binary (and multi-class) classification algorithm despite its name — it models P(class|x) with cross-entropy loss. The word "regression" here refers to fitting a linear model in log-odds space, not to a continuous target.

items.title: Loss functions
items.description: Problem type determines loss: MSE/MAE for regression, cross-entropy for classification. Mismatched loss (e.g. MSE on class IDs) usually signals a mis-framed problem or wrong output head.
